title: Event Loop and Async Code
content:
Welcome to Lecture 6: Event Loop and Async Code. In this chapter, we are going to dive deep into how NodeJS actually works under the hood. We will cover Event-Driven programming, why JavaScript is single-threaded, the difference between the V8 Engine and Libuv, and how to write non-blocking code. Understanding these concepts will make it clear why we use callbacks and how Node handles thousands of requests so efficiently.

1. Event-Driven Programming

In the last chapter, we used the .on() method for 'data' and 'end' events. This is the heart of Node.

  • Intuition: Imagine you are in school. You are "listening" for the school bell. The Event is the bell ringing. Your Event Handler (the code that runs) is packing your bag and leaving.
  • How it works: When an event occurs (like data arriving from a user), it goes into a Queue. The Event Loop picks it up and executes the Callback function you provided.

2. Single-Threaded Nature

NodeJS is Single-Threaded, meaning it has only one "hand" or one unit of execution. It processes everything in a line (Queue). You might wonder: "If there is only one thread, how does it not get stuck?"

3. The Thread Pool (The "Manager" Analogy)

The Event Loop acts like a Manager. A good manager doesn't do all the work; they assign tasks.

  • Offloading: When a heavy task comes in (like reading a file or a database call), the Manager doesn't do it. Instead, they hand it to a Worker Thread in the Thread Pool.
  • Callbacks: The Manager gives the worker a Callback ("Tell me when you are finished"). Once the worker is done, they put a message back into the Event Queue for the Manager to process. This allows Node to handle many tasks at once without blocking the main thread.

4. V8 Engine vs. Libuv

Node is made of two main parts:

  1. V8 Engine: Built by Google, it converts your JavaScript code into Machine Code so the computer hardware can understand it.
  2. Libuv: A C++ library that handles all the "magic"—the Event Loop, the Thread Pool, and the file/network operations. It ensures Node works the same way on Windows, Mac, and Linux.
  • The Master: While V8 runs the JS, Libuv is the actual "master" that manages the schedule and workers.

5. Node Runtime in Action

Consider this code sequence:

  1. console.log('Start')
  2. DB Query (Async Callback)
  3. console.log('End')

You might think it prints in that order, but it doesn't.

  • The first log runs immediately.
  • V8 sees the DB query, realizes it's an Async Operation, and hands it to Libuv. V8 then moves to the next line immediately.
  • The second log ('End') prints.
  • Later, when Libuv finishes the DB query, the callback is put in the Event Queue, and the result prints last.

6. The Event Loop Phases (Order of Priority)

The Event Loop doesn't just spin randomly; it follows a strict order of priority in each "tick" (cycle):

  1. Timers: Checks if setTimeout or setInterval are ready.
  2. Pending Callbacks: Finishes I/O tasks that were delayed from the last cycle.
  3. Idle/Prepare: Internal Node cleanup.
  4. Poll: Retrieves new incoming data/requests (where Node spends most of its time).
  5. Check: Runs setImmediate callbacks.
  6. Close Callbacks: Cleans up closed connections or files.

7. The "Undefined" Trap (Async Code Example)

In our Sum Calculator project, we saw that if we sent the response outside the callback, the sum was undefined.

  • The Reason: Because request.on('end', ...) is Asynchronous, Node starts the process of gathering data but moves past it immediately. If you send the response outside, it sends before the data has finished arriving.
  • The Fix: You must send the response inside the 'end' callback so it only sends once the calculation is complete.

8. Blocking vs. Non-Blocking Code

  • Blocking (fs.writeFileSync): This tells the Manager (Event Loop), "You do this yourself!" The Manager stops everything else to write the file, making all other users wait. This is bad for performance.
  • Non-Blocking (fs.writeFile): This is the correct way. It hands the task to a worker thread and keeps the Event Loop free.
  • Proper Flow: Always put your redirection (res.statusCode = 302) inside the file-writing callback. Otherwise, you might tell the user "Success" before the file is actually finished being written.

9. Exercise: Run and Observe

To master this, dry-run the following logic:

  • Exercise 1: Sync code always runs in order, but async callbacks (even with 0ms delay) always run after the main script finishes.
  • Exercise 2 (Priority): Microtasks (Promises) have the highest priority and run even before Timers. Timers come next, followed by setImmediate and then I/O callbacks.

Summary: By using non-blocking code and understanding the Event Loop, you allow Node to be incredibly fast despite being single-threaded. In the next chapter, we will learn about NPM and Tools to make our development even easier!
